senses.definition: Relating to substances that produce psychotic-like effects or mimic psychosis.
examples: LSD is often described as having psychotomimetic properties.
Researchers studied psychotomimetic drugs to understand their impact on perception.

senses.definition: In a way that imitates or induces psychotic-like states or symptoms.
examples: The drug altered perception psychotomimetically.
Participants experienced mood changes psychotomimetically during the trial.

senses.definition: Substances that produce symptoms similar to psychosis; psychotomimetic drugs.
examples: Researchers studied various psychotomimetics to understand their effects on perception.
The class includes several hallucinogens with psychotomimetic properties.

senses.definition: Toxic to the mind; harmful to mental processes or psychological function.
examples: Chronic exposure to certain substances can be psychotoxic.
Researchers warn about psychotoxic effects in some experimental drugs.

senses.definition: A genus of tropical shrubs and small trees in the coffee family, not commonly used in everyday language.
examples: The botanist collected samples from the Psychotria grove.
Ecologists noted Psychotria species in the understory of the forest.

senses.definition: A rarely used term; may appear as a historical or obscure chemical name not widely adopted in modern lexicon.
examples: Historical texts mention psychotrine in early pharmacology notes.

senses.definition: Relating to the effect of drugs on mood, perception, or behavior, or to drugs that influence mental state.
examples: Psychotropic medications can help treat mood disorders.
The study looked at psychotropic effects of various compounds.

senses.definition: A drug that affects the mind or behavior; a psychotropic substance.
examples: The patient was prescribed a new psychotropic for anxiety.
Authorities regulate psychotropics differently from other medicines.

senses.definition: Multiple psychotropic drugs; substances that affect mood, perception, or behavior.
examples: Antidepressants and antipsychotics are common psychotropics.
Psychotropics are used to treat various mental health conditions.

senses.definition: Uncommon term; not widely used in standard medical or psychological literature.
examples: The concept was described as psychovital in a niche theoretical text.

senses.definition: A rare, possibly coined term; not established in standard dictionaries.
examples: The paper used psychozoic to describe a mental-ecological phase.

senses.definition: A medical term referring to a sensation of cold in the skin or body.
examples: The patient reported psychroesthesia in the fingertips.

senses.definition: A device that records or measures cold-related phenomena; an obscure term not common in general use.
examples: The psychrograph recorded ambient temperature changes over time.

senses.definition: An instrument for measuring humidity by using two thermometers to compare temperatures.
examples: The meteorologist used a psychrometer to estimate relative humidity.
A psychrometer is part of a sling psychrometer set.

senses.definition: Plural of psychrometer; multiple instruments used to measure humidity.
examples: The field team carried several psychrometers for accurate readings.
Calibrated psychrometers improve humidity measurements.

senses.definition: Relating to the measurement of humidity and air moisture content.
examples: Psychrometric charts are used in HVAC design.
A psychrometric analysis helps determine cooling load.

senses.definition: Relating to psychrometry; a rarely used form of the adjective.
examples: The psychrometrical data were graphed for the report.

senses.definition: Plural form of psychrometry; methods or results of measuring humidity.
examples: The psychrometries of the samples were compared across conditions.

senses.definition: The science of measuring humidity in air, often using a psychrometer.
examples: Psychrometry is important in building climate control.
Students study psychrometry in HVAC courses.

senses.definition: An organism that grows best at very cold temperatures.
examples: Psychrophiles thrive in polar oceans.
Some psychrophiles can grow at 0 degrees Celsius.

senses.definition: Loving or thriving in very cold temperatures; adapted to cold.
examples: Psychrophilic bacteria can survive in glaciers.
These enzymes are psychrophilic and function at low temperatures.

senses.definition: An extreme fear of cold or cold environments; rare in common usage.
examples: The expedition team considered psychrophobia as a potential risk.

senses.definition: A term occasionally used in biology or pharmacology to describe a substance carrying a mental effect; not common in standard usage.
examples: Some researchers referenced a psychophore concept in early studies.

senses.definition: An obscure plant or organism associated with mental or psychological terminology in some texts.
examples: Historical botanists mention psychrophytes in quirky classifications.

senses.definition: Therapies involving cold exposure or other cold-related methods; rare in modern practice.
examples: The clinic offered psychrotherapies as a complementary approach.

senses.definition: Slang for psychotropic drugs or substances; short for psychotropics; also a casual nickname for psychologists in some contexts.
examples: The group discussed which psychs to avoid.
Some teenagers think all psychs are dangerous.
